---
title: "repos clone"
---

`repos clone` reads your `repos.list` file and clones all listed repositories
into the **parent directory** of your current location.  It is the simplest
way to get started — no GitHub account or token required for public repositories.

## Basic Usage

```bash
repos clone
```

## Options

| Flag | Description |
|---|---|
| `-f <file>` | Use a different list file (default: `repos.list`) |

```bash
# Use a custom list file
repos clone -f my-repos.list
```

## Repository Layout

Repositories are cloned to the **parent** of your current directory:

```
workspace/
├── my-project/          # your project (contains repos.list)
├── backend/             # cloned from myorg/backend
├── frontend-develop/    # cloned from myorg/frontend@develop
└── docs/                # cloned from myorg/docs
```

## What's in repos.list?

`repos.list` lists the repositories (and optionally branches) to clone.
See the [repos.list reference](repos-list.html) for the full format, including
cloning specific branches, creating worktrees, and setting per-repo visibility.

## GitHub Authentication

For **private** repositories, provide a GitHub token:

```bash
export GH_TOKEN="your_personal_access_token"
# or
gh auth login
```

Public repositories can be cloned without any token.

## Going further with repos setup

`repos clone` only clones repositories.  `repos setup` does everything
`repos clone` does, plus:

- **Creates** any GitHub repositories listed in `repos.list` that do not
  exist yet (requires `GH_TOKEN`)
- **Generates** a VS Code multi-root workspace file
  (`entire-project.code-workspace`)
- **Configures** GitHub Codespaces authentication (with `--codespaces`)

You can also run these extra steps individually after cloning:

- Generate the VS Code workspace: `repos workspace`
- Run all steps at once: `repos setup`

→ [repos setup guide](setup.html)
